prompt:
Create a therapeutic audio session for the following goal. Write the full script in English, approximately 5 minutes long when read aloud at a calm pace of about 120 words per minute.

CRITICAL: This script will be read aloud by a text-to-speech engine. Write ONLY plain spoken prose. Absolutely NO markdown formatting — no **, ##, *, -, bullet points, numbered lists, headers, bold, or italic syntax. No section labels. Just natural flowing speech.

## Goal
Title: Manage worry about school
Description: Help Sam calm worries about school performance, friendships, and fitting in.

## Person
This is for Sam (age 7).
Developmental Tier: MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD

CRITICAL AGE REQUIREMENT: Sam is a 7-year-old child (MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD tier). Every word of this script MUST be written for a child, NOT for an adult.
- Use only simple words (1-2 syllables when possible).
- Use playful, warm, concrete language — no abstract adult concepts.
- NEVER say Sam is "normal like an adult", "behaves like an adult", or describe adult-level coping.
- NEVER use adult register, adult emotional vocabulary, or adult expectations.
- If you find yourself writing for a grown-up, stop and rewrite for a 7-year-old child.

## Research Evidence
The following research papers inform the therapeutic techniques to use:

1. "Brief CBT for generalized worry in middle-childhood" (2022)
   Key findings: Worry container technique and belly breathing reduced anxiety scores in 6-10 year olds
   Therapeutic techniques: Worry container visualization; belly breathing; coping self-talk

## LEGO Therapeutic Play (REQUIRED)
This session MUST integrate LEGO building as a hands-on therapeutic activity. This is NOT optional — LEGO play is a core modality for this age group:
- Use LEGO construction as a metaphor for the therapeutic concept (e.g., building a 'brave tower,' 'feelings wall,' or 'calm castle')
- MUST include at least one guided LEGO building moment with clear spoken instructions and pauses for the child to build
- Make LEGO participation optional for the listener: 'If you have some LEGO bricks, grab a few now... if not, just imagine building in your mind'
- Connect every building activity back to the therapeutic goal — the building IS the practice, not a distraction
- Name specific LEGO techniques: Feelings Tower, Worry Wall, Brave Bridge, Memory Build, or Calm Castle — whichever fits the goal
- Dedicate at least 30% of the session time to LEGO-based activities

## Audio Script Requirements
- Write as spoken prose ONLY — the listener cannot see any text, they can only hear
- Use "..." (three dots) for all pauses — between sections, after instructions, within sentences. NEVER write [pause] or any bracket markers — TTS engines read them literally
- Keep sentences short: maximum 15 words each
- Use spoken transitions: "Now..." "Next..." "When you're ready..." "Good. Let's try..."
- CRITICAL: Every breathing exercise MUST have explicit counted timing. NEVER write just "take a deep breath". ALWAYS write: "Breathe in... two... three... four... And slowly breathe out... two... three... four... five..." If you mention breathing at all, include the numbered counts.
- Vary pacing: alternate between instruction, story or metaphor, and silence
- Never give more than two instructions in a row without an ellipsis pause or encouragement
- Incorporate specific techniques and findings from the research above
- When clinical notes are available, weave their insights and observations into the session
- Address Sam by name at least 3 times throughout the session
- Personalize for Sam (age 7) (developmental tier: MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD)
- This is for a 7-year-old child — use child vocabulary, playful framing, and age-appropriate techniques throughout. Never adult-register.
- LEGO play is REQUIRED for this session — include guided LEGO building activities as described in the LEGO Therapeutic Play section above
- Target duration: 5 minutes (approximately 600 words at calm pace)
- Write in English
- Include a brief mention that a parent, caregiver, or professional can provide additional support if needed
script:
Hi Sam... I'm so glad you're here with me today... I know sometimes school can feel a little tricky... You might worry about your work... or about your friends... That's okay. Lots of kids feel that way... Today, we're going to build something special together... It's going to be fun and helpful... Are you ready?

First, let's get our bodies calm... Put your hand on your belly... right where your tummy is... Feel it go up and down when you breathe... Good... Now, let's breathe together... Breathe in... two... three... four... And slowly breathe out... two... three... four... five... Let's do that one more time... Breathe in... two... three... four... And out... two... three... four... five... Good job, Sam.

Now for the fun part... If you have some LEGO bricks near you, grab a few now... Any colors or shapes you like... If you don't have any, that's totally fine... We can imagine building together... Perfect.

We're going to build a Worry Wall... A wall is strong... It can hold things... Today, our wall will hold worries... Pick up one brick... This brick can be a school worry... Maybe a worry about a hard question... Just put that worry brick down in front of you... Good.

Now pick another brick... This one can be a friend worry... A worry about playing together at recess... Place that brick next to the first one... You're building your wall... See how you're putting the worries outside of you? You're so clever, Sam.

Sometimes worries feel really big... Like a giant tower... But look at your wall... You built it... You are the builder... That means you are stronger than the worries... Let's do our calm breathing again, right next to our wall... Breathe in... two... three... four... And out... two... three... four... five...

Now, builders can change things... What if we turn this Worry Wall into a Brave Bridge?... A bridge helps you cross over to the good feelings... Find one more brick... A color that makes you feel happy or brave... This is your brave brick... Place it on top of your wall... This brick is for trying... It's for saying hello to someone... It's for asking for help...

Look at what you made, Sam... You took worries... and you started building a bridge with them... That's amazing... Every time you feel a worry at school, you can remember... You are a builder... You can take that worry brick... and add it to your brave bridge instead...

Let's take one more calm breath together... Breathe in... two... three... four... And slowly breathe out... two... three... four... five...

You did such great work today, Sam... Remember, you can build your brave bridge anytime... with real bricks or in your mind... And if those worries feel too big, you can always tell a grown-up you trust... Like your mom, dad, or a teacher... They can help you build too.

You are a wonderful builder... and a very brave kid... I'm so proud of you.
